framework.reasoning: Paper's model family (Qwen2.5/Qwen3/Mistral) and the GRPO training lineage it builds on live entirely in the PyTorch/HuggingFace ecosystem. Using HuggingFace TRL's existing GRPOTrainer (rather than re-implementing GRPO's clipped policy-gradient loop from scratch) avoids re-deriving a well-established RL algorithm on top of an already-scoped-down Colab/Kaggle compute budget — the paper's actual novel contribution is the reward-shaping term (Eq. 4/6), not GRPO itself, so effort should concentrate there. LoRA (via PEFT) + 4-bit loading (via bitsandbytes) are included specifically to fit RL fine-tuning in a free-tier GPU's memory (per SIR ambiguity: paper's own experiments used full fine-tuning on non-free-tier hardware).

module_hierarchy.description: Wraps a small HF causal LM (policy + frozen/reference copy) with optional LoRA adapters and 4-bit quantization.
Verifier for the Countdown task: parses a generated equation, checks it uses each given number exactly once and evaluates to the target.

public_methods.name: score
public_methods.signature: (self, numbers: list[int], target: int, completion: str) -> float

module_hierarchy.description: Implements Eq. 4 / Eq. 6 — the paper's core contribution: shifts the raw reward/advantage using the reference model's own log-probability, asymmetrically for correct vs. incorrect trajectories.

public_methods.name: shape_advantage
public_methods.signature: (self, advantages: torch.Tensor, is_correct: torch.Tensor, ref_logprobs: torch.Tensor, gamma_p: float, gamma_n: float) -> torch.Tensor

module_hierarchy.description: Group-relative advantage normalization (Eq. 5), shared by both vanilla GRPO and DS-GRPO.

Thin wrapper around TRL's GRPOTrainer that injects the DifferentialSmoothingShaper as a reward/advantage post-processing hook; toggled on/off via config so vanilla-GRPO and DS-GRPO share identical code paths otherwise.

public_methods.name: compute_loss
public_methods.returns: policy-gradient loss with optional differential smoothing applied

module_hierarchy.description: Procedurally generates Countdown puzzles (3-4 integers in [1,99], target reachable via +-*/), no download required.
Pass@1, Pass@K, and Solution Multiplicity (Eq. 2) computed over sampled completions.

public_methods.name: pass_at_k
public_methods.signature: (self, completions: list[list[str]], verifier: CountdownVerifier, k: int) -> float

public_methods.name: solution_multiplicity
public_methods.returns: avg distinct correct solutions per problem (Eq. 2)

module_hierarchy.description: YAML config loading + global seeding (Python/NumPy/PyTorch) for reproducibility.

tensor_flows.name: DS-GRPO training step
steps: policy.generate() samples G completions per prompt -> [B, G] completions
policy.logprob() / reference.logprob() on sampled completions -> ref_logprobs [B, G]
DifferentialSmoothingShaper.shape_advantage(advantages, is_correct, ref_logprobs, gamma_p, gamma_n) -> A_i^DS [B, G] (Eq. 6) — SKIPPED if running vanilla-GRPO baseline config
DSGRPOTrainer.compute_loss() -> clipped policy-gradient loss + KL term (Eq. 1) -> policy_loss

model.base_checkpoint: Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct  # ASSUMED: scoped down from paper's Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct (Countdown setup) to fit a free Kaggle/Colab GPU; confidence 0.3, override to Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct if a larger free GPU is available

training.learning_rate: 1e-6  # ASSUMED: not stated in paper (SIR ambiguity, confidence 0.5)
training.optimizer: adamw  # ASSUMED: not stated in paper
training.num_train_steps: 300  # ASSUMED: paper trains 'until reward saturates' with no fixed count; scoped down for free-tier compute budget
training.kl_beta: 0.01  # ASSUMED: paper studies a range but no single default given for main results (confidence 0.4)

runtime.reason: provides GRPOTrainer base implementation to extend, avoids re-deriving GRPO from scratch
LoRA adapters to fit fine-tuning in limited GPU memory
HF Dataset wrapper convenience for the procedurally-generated Countdown set

entrypoints.purpose: Train either vanilla-GRPO or DS-GRPO on the Countdown task, controlled by a config flag, so both share identical code otherwise.

cli_args.help: toggle DS-GRPO vs vanilla GRPO baseline
tiny run for quick local testing

entrypoints.purpose: Compute Pass@1, Pass@K, and Solution Multiplicity on a held-out set of Countdown puzzles for a trained checkpoint.
Run the trained model on a single user-provided Countdown puzzle and print the generated solution(s).

risk_assessment.description: Even with LoRA + 4-bit loading, RL fine-tuning requires holding the policy model, a reference/old-policy copy, and G sampled rollouts' activations simultaneously — could still exceed a free T4/P100's ~16GB VRAM depending on group_size and sequence length.
risk_assessment.source: training_pipeline confidence 0.35 in SIR; free-tier compute constraint
risk_assessment.mitigation: Start with the smallest model (0.5B), small group_size (8), short sequences; increase only if memory allows. Reduce group_size or switch to gradient checkpointing if OOM.

risk_assessment.description: Training hyperparameters (LR, optimizer, step count) are assumed defaults, not paper-verified — if the assumed values are far off, neither vanilla-GRPO nor DS-GRPO may train stably.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Run a short --dry-run and a tiny --debug pass first to confirm loss decreases sensibly before committing to a full run; be prepared to adjust learning_rate by 2-5x if training diverges or stalls.

risk_assessment.description: The Countdown verifier (correctness checker) is a from-scratch reimplementation since the paper doesn't specify its exact parsing/validation logic — a bug here would silently corrupt every reward signal.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Unit-test the verifier directly on hand-written example equations (known-correct and known-incorrect) before using it in training.

risk_assessment.description: A very small base model (0.5B) may already perform poorly or randomly on Countdown regardless of RL method, making it harder to cleanly observe the sharpening-vs-smoothing effect the paper describes.
risk_assessment.source: scope-down decision (model swapped from paper's 3B to 0.5B)
risk_assessment.mitigation: If the 0.5B model shows no learning signal at all, step up to Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct before concluding the method doesn't work.
